title: Lesson 434
transcript: All right. You might see something familiar. I'm finally wearing the same sweatshirt that Rob usually wears. So we look like a team finally. Not the exact same one. Let's get that clear. But yeah, we look like a team. Isn't that nice? We're getting you to the top of the CISSP mountain. All right. We're in domain number four, of course, which is focused on network security. And as part of network security, every organization has to protect against network attacks. That's what this section will deal with. Now, you know, we could spend weeks talking about how to protect against specific network attacks that exists out there, right? And there are many and so on, so that's not really the focus of the CISSP exam. Obviously, they could ask you about specific attacks and so on, but as usual, let's frame our minds on what we know is specifically mentioned in the exam outline, where the questions ultimately come from. and also what we know people are seeing, right? The first thing that we have to focus on from a network security perspective and therefore protecting against network attacks is that if we understand the phases of a successful attack then the whole focus of preventing against network attacks is to make each of these phases impossible or more difficult for an attacker to actually achieve. And you'll remember earlier in this course, we talked about something called the cyber kill chain. Right, something that the industry has been focusing on for a number of years now. And that is you wanna break that chain so that successful attacks cannot be completed. It's the same concept here. In fact, when you look at these phases here, reconnaissance, enumeration, vulnerability analysis, and exploitation. These are the phases that any successful attack has to go through and they have to be completed in the order that you see here. So the idea is that if you know the successful phases that a successful attack needs to go through, the focus from a network security perspective is to make each of these more difficult or impossible for an attacker to complete. So for example, if you understand that the first phase in any successful attack is reconnaissance, then you want to make that phase as difficult as possible. You'll remember that this is where the attackers are looking for initial gathering of information relating to potential targets. They're looking for things like domain names and IP address ranges and so on. So the idea is you don't want to make this easy for any potential attacker. So you don't publish your domain names and IP address ranges. You don't want to make yourself an attractive target. All right, so making each of these more difficult is a good strategy in preventing successful attacks. The other thing here, like for example, second phase here is enumeration. This is where the attackers are looking for specific information. They're looking for, ports and services that may be running, versions of operating systems, and versions of technologies and architectures that you may be using inside of your organization that they would understand may have potential vulnerabilities that ultimately they can exploit. The idea here is, again, you want to be able to understand all of your architectures. and systems and make sure that, for example, we harden them to the point where anything that is not needed is not running. And one of the really good things that you can do here is you prevent people that work for your organization in providing that type of information out there. So you have to have policies that talk about how your people cannot post things on news groups. and forums and up on social media websites like LinkedIn, for example, the experience that they have in working with specific technologies inside of your organization. These are obviously, you know, places that attackers can look for. and understand exactly the technologies and the architectures that you may be using inside of your organization, which may allow them to take advantage of each of these phases here so that ultimately they can potentially conduct successful attacks. So the whole idea of this, and you don't have to memorize all of this and so on, but making each of these phases difficult and impossible should be the focus of network security in any organization. Now, something that the exam does require you to understand is the difference between passive and active attacks. Now, there's an easy way to understand the difference here. And that is to look at it from the perspective of detection. All right, because passive attacks are impossible or very difficult to detect. Whereas active attacks, are very easily detectable, right? So if the exam asks you questions, is this attack a example of a passive or active attack? Look at it from the perspective of the organization itself being able to detect that attack. If it is detectable, it is a active attack. If it is not detectable, then it is a passive type of an attack. Now again, we could spend weeks talking about different types of attacks here, but we're only gonna focus on the ones that we know are specifically mentioned. in the exam outline. So you'll see a few slides here coming up that we know people are seeing questions on from the perspective of understanding whether those attacks are passive or active. But before we get to that, You see another set of terms here, threat agents. We would have talked about threat agents back in domain number one, when we talked about risk management. We said a threat agent is something or someone that can introduce risks into a environment, right? So a perfect example of a threat agent is an attacker because that attacker can introduce certain exploits and vulnerabilities into an architecture that they may be able to take advantage of. So think about threat agent being someone or something that can introduce risks and threats into an environment such as Now let's go back to these concepts here. And again, let's look at these from the perspective of being able to detect. That's the easy way to understand. whether an attack is passive or active. So here's an example, eavesdropping, which is defined as picking up something that is not meant for you. So for example, you could be at a restaurant and you could be eavesdropping on someone else's conversation. You're not doing anything, you're not interrupting them, you're simply listening. In fact, the word passive here gives it away as to what kind of an attack this is. Specifically related to networks, if you have someone that is eavesdropping or picking up traffic that's going across the network that is not meant for them, that is something that is very difficult for an organization to pick up or detect. Therefore, Passively sniffing or eavesdropping on network traffic is considered to be a passive type of an attack. Now, I know some very technical people will argue with this. Right, but I'll explain it here. But some people say, well, in order to be able to pick up all traffic across a network, for example, you need to put your network card into promiscuous mode. And that is something that may be detectable by others. And you'd be absolutely correct in saying that. But the exam doesn't get to that level of detail. So if you're simply picking up information without changing it, or doing anything with it, you're simply eavesdropping on that traffic, then that is something that is very difficult or impossible to detect. Therefore, the exam considers that, just like the industry does, to be a passive type of an attack, right? Here's the next one. And I guess the word actively gives this one away as well. But let's say that an attacker is potentially looking for ports and services that may be running on certain architectures or hosts, for example, so that they may be able to understand certain exploits and vulnerabilities that they can take advantage of. that requires the attacker to be able to probe or send packets to that host, for example, which is something that the organization would be able to detect. And therefore, this is considered to be a active type of an attack. Because again, the attacker needs to send something to that host to be able to determine whether that port or service is actually running. So this is detectable from the perspective of the organization. It is an active type of an attack. In fact, here's a specific example of that. It's referred to as SYN scanning, S-Y-N scanning. This refers back to what we talked about earlier, and that is TCP connections. But let's understand the scenario. This is where an attacker sends a synchronized request to a host. at a specific port number, which as you know equates to services. Depending on the information that that attacker gets back, they will be able to determine whether that service or port is open And in fact, more than that, because depending on the response that they may get back, it may also tell them whether there's a stateful firewall that sits in between. All right, so this is very valuable information. that obviously attackers are looking for. But because they're sending packets to the host, we can detect this and therefore this would also be considered to be a active type of an attack. Depending on the information that obviously they get back, they know whether something is exploitable or not. And here's another one too. In fact, we referred to this earlier when we talked about TCP connections, right? We talked about how TCP connections start with that three-way handshake which starts with a synchronized request. And then we said something like, what if a host is receiving a whole bunch of synchronized requests, but they're coming faster than the host can actually handle? What happens is the in-process queue or connection queue will slowly fill up because you're receiving as a host, for example, you're receiving synchronized requests faster than you can handle so therefore your in process also referred to as the connection queue slowly fills up and when it does fill up the host is going to say oh i can't do this i need to shut down it's a denial of service attack which is very appropriately referred to as a sin flood attack And we said the best way to protect against that is to not let the host handle synchronized requests on its own. Let's create an application, which we can call a proxy, which can handle synchronized requests. And because it's an application, It'll never go down. It can be smart enough to understand that we're getting flooded by synchronized requests and we can just ignore them. A SYN proxy is what we can call that. But any denial of service attack, which is what this is an example of, is obviously something that is detectable because it obviously changes the state of, in this case, the target server, for example. And it requires, obviously, sending of packets to that server, which can be detectable. And therefore, it would also be considered to be an active type of an attack. So any denial of service attack is going to be detectable and therefore also considered to be an active type of an attack.
